title: Insecure Authentication
description: Authentication failures occur when applications implement credential storage, session management, or identity verification incorrectly, allowing attackers to compromise passwords, hijack sessions, or bypass login entirely.
learning_objectives: Explain why MD5 and SHA1 are unsuitable for password storage
Implement bcrypt or Argon2 for secure password hashing
Design secure session token generation and management
Apply rate limiting and account lockout to prevent brute force
Recognise weak authentication patterns in AI-generated login code

sections.title: How Authentication Failures Occur
sections.content: Authentication failures arise from a range of implementation mistakes: storing passwords in plain text or using fast, cryptographically broken hash functions; failing to invalidate sessions on logout; using predictable or short session tokens; and omitting rate limiting that allows unlimited login attempts. Any one of these mistakes can render an otherwise well-designed application vulnerable to account compromise.
sections.key_concept: Passwords must never be recoverable — even by the application itself. A secure system can verify a password without ever being able to retrieve it.

sections.title: Real-World Attack Scenario
sections.scenario: An online learning platform stores passwords as MD5 hashes. An attacker gains read access to the database through an unrelated vulnerability and exports the users table. Using freely available rainbow tables — precomputed databases of MD5 hashes for millions of common passwords — the attacker reverses 70% of all hashes within minutes. Because many users reuse passwords, the attacker then uses these credentials against email providers and banking sites.
impact: Mass account compromise following a database breach
Credential stuffing attacks against other services the user relies on
Account takeover without ever interacting with the login form
Persistent access if sessions are not invalidated after breach discovery

vulnerable.problem: MD5 was designed for speed — a GPU can compute billions of MD5 hashes per second. An attacker with the hash database can brute-force common passwords in seconds.

secure.fix: bcrypt is deliberately slow and automatically incorporates a random salt. Each password gets a unique hash, defeating rainbow tables, and the cost factor can be increased as hardware improves.

vulnerable.code:
# VULNERABLE: predictable session token
import hashlib
import time

def create_session(user_id):
    # Predictable: based on user_id and current time
    token = hashlib.md5(
        f'{user_id}{time.time()}'.encode()
    ).hexdigest()
    sessions[token] = user_id
    return token

# An attacker who knows approximate login time
# can brute-force the token space
vulnerable.problem: Session tokens derived from predictable values (user ID, timestamp) can be guessed or brute-forced, allowing session hijacking without stealing the token.

secure.code:
# SECURE: cryptographically random session token
import secrets

def create_session(user_id: int) -> str:
    # secrets.token_urlsafe uses os.urandom() — truly random
    # 32 bytes = 256 bits of entropy — computationally infeasible to guess
    token = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
    sessions[token] = {
        'user_id': user_id,
        'created_at': time.time(),
        'expires_at': time.time() + 3600  # 1 hour
    }
    return token

def invalidate_session(token: str):
    # Always remove session on logout
    sessions.pop(token, None)
secure.fix: secrets.token_urlsafe() uses a cryptographically secure random number generator. With 256 bits of entropy, guessing a token is statistically impossible.

sections.title: Security-by-Design Principles
sections.content: Secure authentication must be designed in from the start. Retrofitting security onto a system that stores plain-text passwords or uses weak sessions requires migrating all existing data — a costly and risky operation.

principles.title: Choose the Right Hashing Algorithm
principles.detail: For passwords, use bcrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2. These are memory-hard or deliberately slow, making brute force impractical. Never use MD5, SHA1, or SHA256 directly for passwords — they are designed to be fast.
principles.example:
# Argon2 — the current gold standard
from argon2 import PasswordHasher
ph = PasswordHasher(time_cost=2, memory_cost=65536, parallelism=2)
hash = ph.hash(password)
ph.verify(hash, password)  # Raises exception if wrong

principles.title: Rate Limiting and Account Lockout
principles.detail: Limit authentication attempts to prevent brute force. After N failed attempts, lock the account temporarily or require CAPTCHA. Implement this server-side — client-side checks can be bypassed.

principles.title: Multi-Factor Authentication
principles.detail: Even with perfectly stored passwords, credentials can be phished. MFA requires a second factor (TOTP app, SMS, hardware key) that an attacker cannot obtain just by stealing the password.

items.text: Store passwords using bcrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2 — never MD5, SHA1, or plain text
Generate session tokens using a cryptographically secure random number generator
Invalidate all sessions on logout and after password changes
Implement rate limiting on login endpoints to prevent brute force
Set session expiry — tokens should not be valid indefinitely
Use HttpOnly and Secure cookie flags for session tokens
Consider multi-factor authentication for sensitive applications

sections.title: Watch Out in AI-Generated Code
sections.content: AI tools frequently generate authentication code using MD5 or SHA256 for password hashing, or derive session tokens from predictable values like timestamps and user IDs. These are among the most commonly generated security vulnerabilities.
red_flags: str(user_id) + str(time.time()) used as a session token
No salt applied before hashing — vulnerable to rainbow tables
Session tokens stored in localStorage (accessible to XSS)

quiz.question: Why is MD5 unsuitable for storing passwords?
options: MD5 produces hashes that are too short to be unique
MD5 is too slow, making login verification impractical
MD5 is a fast hash function, making brute-force attacks computationally cheap
MD5 is not supported by modern databases
quiz.explanation: MD5 was designed for speed — modern hardware can compute billions of MD5 hashes per second. This makes it trivial to brute-force common passwords. Password hashing algorithms like bcrypt are intentionally slow to make brute force impractical.

quiz.question: What additional security property does bcrypt provide beyond slowing down hashing?
options: It encrypts the password so it can be recovered by admins
It automatically incorporates a unique random salt for each password
It compresses the hash to save database storage
It validates password complexity before hashing
quiz.explanation: bcrypt generates a unique random salt and incorporates it into the hash for every password. This means two users with the same password have different hashes — rainbow table attacks, which rely on precomputed hashes, are defeated entirely.

quiz.question: Which function should be used to generate a secure session token in Python?
quiz.explanation: secrets.token_urlsafe(32) uses Python's cryptographically secure random number generator to produce 256 bits of entropy. UUID4 is random but only has 122 bits of entropy and is sometimes predictable. MD5 and timestamp-based tokens are predictable and guessable.
